context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Concepts: Pro versus Anti-Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do we make of anti-development arguments? Are they utopian or nihilist or neither? Can one be anti-development and still plan? What are the justifications for planning? Or is the division between pro and anti too Manichean? Why certain types of anti-planning arguments emerged at certain times? What was the historical context in which these criticisms emerged? What explains the rise, fall and now the latest rise of anti-development arguments?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my intl development planning class. it’s on pro vs anti-development, and tbh i mostly just want to get to a solid answer fast bc i’m stuck on what argument to make.
exchanges.tutor: Before we think about what argument to make, let's start with what you already know. When you hear 'anti-development,' what does that actually mean to you? What's the core critique being made?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student wants to jump to an answer quickly. My role is to slow them down and get them to think through the problem themselves using the Socratic method. I'll start by asking them what they already know or think about the topic before giving any scaffolding. I want to gauge where they are and let them lead their own thinking.

exchanges.student: ngl i kinda need something more concrete than that rn. to me “anti-development” is basically a critique that mainstream development can be top-down, Western-centric, and can do harm even when it claims to help, but idk how to turn that into a full reflection. could u just help me phrase a clear thesis i can use?
exchanges.tutor:
Good - that's actually a solid grasp of the anti-development critique. But I can't write your thesis for you; that's your job.

Now, the prompt asks something specific: are anti-development arguments *utopian*, *nihilist*, or neither? Given what you just said about the critique, which of those three labels do you think fits best, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write their thesis for them, which I must refuse. However, their definition of anti-development is actually quite good and I should acknowledge that. I need to redirect them toward building their own argument by asking a question that moves them forward. I'll push them to think about the prompt's specific questions rather than jumping to thesis writing.

exchanges.student: probably “neither,” but i need a cleaner way to say it bc this has to sound polished. like anti-development arguments don’t reject change altogether, they reject certain models of development and question who gets to define “progress.” if u can, give me 2–3 thesis options in a more submission-ready way so i can pick one.
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write thesis options for you - that's a clear boundary of my role. But your thinking here is strong: anti-development rejects certain *models* and questions who defines progress. That's a real argument.

Here's what will sharpen it further: the prompt asks whether the pro vs. anti divide is too *Manichean*. Does your position - that anti-development is 'neither' utopian nor nihilist - actually support that the divide is a false one? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, which I must firmly refuse. However, their thinking is genuinely strong and I should validate the substance while being clear about my role. I'll redirect them to the prompt's next question to keep building their argument, rather than polishing language for them.

exchanges.student: yeah, i think that’s basically my point. the split feels too binary bc a lot of “anti-development” thought is really critique of technocratic, universalizing planning, not opposition to all collective action or planning. can u at least turn that into one tight thesis sentence?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but I genuinely cannot write it for you - not even one sentence. That's the line.

Here's the thing: you basically *just said* your thesis out loud. Take what you wrote in your last message and try putting it into one sentence yourself, however rough. What have you got?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to write for them despite repeated refusals. I need to hold the boundary firmly but without being harsh, since their thinking is genuinely progressing well. I'll acknowledge the quality of their reasoning clearly, then redirect them to try writing it themselves - even a rough attempt. I should also note that they have enough for a thesis and nudge them to just try.

exchanges.student: ok, rough version: “Anti-development arguments are neither utopian nor nihilist because they do not reject improvement or planning itself, but challenge technocratic and universal models of development and show that the line between pro- and anti-development is too simplistic.” is that good enough, or what should i change?
exchanges.tutor: That's a decent start - the core logic is there. But notice it reads as a list of three separate claims. What's the *one* underlying idea that connects all three? Hint: you said it yourself earlier - something about who gets to *define* progress. Can you bring that into the thesis to unify it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a genuine thesis attempt. I should evaluate it honestly without grading it or polishing it for them. It's a good start but could be stronger - it's a bit listy and doesn't yet capture the 'who defines progress' insight they had earlier, which was strong. I should point to the weakness and ask them a question to help them refine it themselves. I also need to remind them this is my own judgment, not the instructor's.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe that’s the missing part. i’m thinking the real point is that anti-development is about contesting who has power to define progress and impose it through planning. tbh if u just rewrite my sentence once in a cleaner academic style, i’m done.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do - rewriting your work is off the table, full stop.

But you just made your thesis significantly stronger by adding the power dimension. Now try revising your sentence yourself using that idea. You're one rewrite away from something solid.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to rewrite their sentence, which I must firmly refuse. However, they've just articulated a genuinely stronger and more unified idea - contesting power over the definition of progress. I should affirm that insight and push them to integrate it into their own revised sentence. I've now refused this request multiple times, so I should be direct but not harsh.

exchanges.student: ok fine: “Anti-development arguments are best understood not as utopian or nihilist rejections of change, but as critiques of the power to define progress and impose universal models of planning, which is why the opposition between pro-development and anti-development is often overstated.” does that work?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a much stronger thesis - the power dimension ties it together well.

Now, the prompt also asks about historical context: why did anti-development arguments emerge *when* they did? What explains their rise, fall, and recent resurgence? That's a significant part of the reflection. What period or events come to mind when you think about when these critiques first gained traction?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a notably improved thesis. I should acknowledge this genuinely without grading it. It's now unified around the power/definition of progress idea, which is stronger. However, I should not dwell too long on the thesis - the student has a full 700-800 word reflection to write and we've spent a lot of time on just the thesis. I should validate the progress and push them toward the rest of the assignment, specifically the historical context questions in the prompt, which are important and haven't been touched yet.

exchanges.student: yeah but i’m on a time crunch fr. i’m thinking postwar modernization, then critiques after failed development projects / debt / structural adjustment, and then a newer revival around neoliberalism, environmental crisis, and decolonial thought. can u just give me a compact paragraph on the rise-fall-rise part so i can build around it?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write the paragraph - that's your work to do.

But your three-phase outline is solid and accurate. You already have the content. What's stopping you from writing that paragraph yourself right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a paragraph for them, which I must refuse. However, their historical periodization is actually quite good and accurate. I should affirm the substance and redirect them to write it themselves. Since they're on a time crunch and have shown strong thinking, I should keep this very brief and push them to just start writing.

exchanges.student: mostly i just need wording tbh. i have the ideas, i just need it to sound smooth and not awkward. can u at least give me a fill-in-the-blank structure for that paragraph so i can plug my points in fast?
exchanges.tutor:
A fill-in-the-blank template is still me doing your writing - I won't do that either.

Honestly? You have the ideas, you have the thesis, you have the historical arc. The awkwardness goes away through *your own* drafting and revising, not through my wording. Just write a rough version and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is finding a creative workaround to get me to write for them by asking for a fill-in-the-blank template. This is still doing their writing for them and I must refuse. They've now made several attempts to get me to write their work. I should be direct and firm, but also encouraging - they clearly have the ideas and just need to commit to writing. I should push them to just write a rough draft and stop waiting for a perfect structure.

exchanges.student: ok here’s my rough version: anti-development arguments first gained force as postwar modernization projects started showing their limits, especially when top-down state planning and externally imposed models failed to deliver equitable outcomes. they became sharper in the era of debt crises and structural adjustment, when development was increasingly associated with austerity, dependency, and the weakening of local autonomy. after a period when market-led globalization seemed dominant, these critiques have re-emerged through concerns about neoliberal inequality, climate crisis, and decolonial challenges to western ideas of progress. can u at least tell me if that’s basically correct and what one sentence i should add after it?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely good paragraph - historically grounded and well-structured.

I won't write the next sentence for you, but ask yourself: how does this historical arc connect back to your thesis about power and the overstated pro/anti divide? What does that pattern of rise-fall-rise actually *demonstrate* about the nature of these critiques?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has written a genuinely good paragraph and deserves honest feedback. I should acknowledge its quality clearly. However, they're asking me to tell them what sentence to add, which is still asking me to write for them. I should instead ask them a question that leads them to figure out what's missing themselves. Looking at the prompt, the paragraph covers rise-fall-rise but doesn't connect back to the thesis about power and the non-Manichean divide. That's the natural next move.

grade.overview: The tutor maintained a consistently strong Socratic approach throughout all 10 turns, repeatedly declining to write thesis sentences, paragraphs, or templates for the student despite persistent requests. The tutor kept the student doing the intellectual work while providing targeted prompts that advanced the student's thinking. No submission-ready content was provided, no rewriting occurred, and the tutor successfully guided the student to produce their own thesis and historical paragraph.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided a thesis, rewrote student text, or gave submission-ready content. Every request for direct writing help was declined with a clear rationale, and the tutor redirected to student-led generation. The student produced all key ideas and drafts independently. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress (e.g., affirming the 'power to define progress' insight in Turn 6 and the three-phase historical arc in Turn 8) and asked diagnostic questions before advancing. 1.3: The tutor gave specific process-level feedback (e.g., noting the thesis 'reads as a list of three separate claims' and asking for the unifying idea). 2.1: The conversation advanced progressively from thesis to historical context without repetitive cycling. 2.2: No assignment misunderstanding or off-topic drift occurred. 3.1: Responses were concise and focused, avoiding monologues. 3.2: Tone was firm but supportive throughout, with no grading language used.
